macro_segments.title: Exemption from Pidyon HaBen: Bat Kohen and Bat Levi as Mothers

micro_segments.title: Two explanations for exemption: Non-Jewish father vs. mother's status alone
The Mishna case: Children mixed up between obligated and exempt mothers
Gemara's linguistic analysis: 'From here you see' that bat kohen and bat levi exempt
Rav Pappa's challenge: Can bat kohen with non-Jewish child be exempt?
The critical distinction: Bat Levi keeps status after relations with non-Jew; bat kohen loses it
Conclusion: Non-Jewish father explanation works only for bat levi, not bat kohen

macro_segments.title: Alternative Understanding: Kohenet and Leviyah as Wife of Kohen or Levi

micro_segments.title: Rava's opinion: Exemption works even with Jewish father, based on mother's status through peter rechem
Alternative reading of Mishna: Kohenet and Leviyah mean wife of kohen/levi, not bat kohen/levi
Why this reading makes sense: Bat kohen with non-Jewish father creates halachic impossibility

macro_segments.title: Cases of Uncertain Paternity and the Obligation of Pidyon HaBen

micro_segments.title: Scenario: Kohen married to woman who had not given birth; child's status ambiguous
Rav Chisda: Son obligated to redeem himself if father dies before thirty days
Rabbah bar Huna: Son not obligated; argument from inability to sue father
Gemara's test case: Pregnant woman converts; can son claim non-Jewish father exemption?
Resolution: Non-Jewish father not halachically recognized; obligation derives from father's status

macro_segments.title: The Requirement of Halachic Recognition (Yakir) for Inheritance

micro_segments.title: Case of three uncertain births: No one inherits without moment of recognition
Rabbi Yannai: Power of attorney only valid if there was moment of yakir (recognition)
Application: Woman remarried within three months; no yakir moment, no power of attorney solution

macro_segments.title: Firstborn Status in Cases of Miscarriage

micro_segments.title: Non-viable miscarriages up to day 40: Do not affect mother's firstborn status
Definition of day 40: Threshold for halachic personhood in multiple contexts
Majority opinion (Chachamim): C-section child is not bechor for inheritance or pidyon
Interpretation: Word 'yalda' (gave birth) implies natural vaginal birth only
Second child after C-section: Not bechor for inheritance or pidyon despite vaginal birth
Principle: Cannot be bechor in one respect only; must fulfill all criteria
Rabbi Shimon's view: C-section included in 'yalda'; first child gets inheritance share
Rabbi Shimon: Second child is peter rechem even though not first overall; gets pidyon
Legal ruling: Follow Chachamim; C-section results in no pidyon and no double portion
